build_guides.name: 5-Inch Freestyle Build — Apex Freestyle
build_guides.description: Complete step-by-step assembly guide for the Apex Freestyle 5-inch quad. Covers frame assembly, motor installation, FC/ESC stack wiring, analog FPV system setup, ELRS receiver binding, and full Betaflight configuration. Designed for intermediate builders with basic soldering skills. By the end of this guide you will have a fully tuned, flight-ready 5-inch freestyle drone.

steps.title: Component Inventory & Workspace Setup
steps.description:
Before touching a soldering iron, lay out every component on a clean, well-lit workspace and verify you have everything needed for the build.

Unbox and inspect each component:
- Frame kit: Verify all carbon plates, standoffs, hardware bags are present. Check for cracks or chips in the carbon.
- Motors (x4): Confirm all 4 are the same KV rating. Spin each bell by hand — it should rotate freely with no grinding or grit.
- Stack (FC+ESC): Inspect the PCB for damaged solder pads or bent pin headers. Check the included hardware bag for rubber grommets and mounting screws.
- Propellers (x4 sets): You need 2 CW and 2 CCW props. Verify both rotation directions are included.
- Camera: Check the lens is clean and undamaged. Note the voltage input range on the label.
- VTX: Verify the antenna connector type (MMCX, U.FL, or SMA).
- Receiver: Check antenna wire length and connector type.
- Battery: Confirm cell count and connector match your build specs.

Pro tip: Take a photo of all components laid out together. If you need to do a warranty claim later, this photo is invaluable. Also photograph the back of each PCB — some components have important pinout diagrams printed on the silkscreen.

steps.title: Frame Assembly — Bottom Plate & Standoffs
steps.description:
Start by assembling the frame's bottom plate with standoffs. This forms the foundation that everything else mounts to.

1. Lay the bottom plate carbon-side up on your workspace. Identify the front (narrower/tapered end on most freestyle frames) and rear.
2. Thread the aluminum standoffs into the bottom plate's center mounting holes. Hand-tighten first, then snug with a hex driver. Do NOT over-torque — carbon fiber can crack around bolt holes.
3. If your frame uses a separate camera cage/bumper, attach it to the front of the bottom plate now. It's much harder to access these bolts once the top plate is on.
4. Install the motor mounting arms if they're separate pieces. Ensure each arm is fully seated and the motor mount bolt holes face outward.
5. Do NOT install the top plate yet — you need open access to the stack area for electronics installation.

Pro tip: Apply a tiny drop of blue Loctite (242) to each standoff bolt. Vibration from motors will loosen hardware over time. Never use red Loctite — you'll never get it apart again.

steps.title: Motor Installation
steps.description:
Mount all four motors to the frame arms. Motor direction will be configured in Betaflight later — physical orientation matters less than secure mounting.

1. For each motor, feed the 3 motor wires through the arm's wire channel or slot (if the frame has one). Many freestyle frames have a channel cut into the arm to protect wires from prop strikes.
2. Align the motor's mounting holes with the arm's holes. 2207 motors on a 5-inch frame use M3 bolts with 16mm spacing.
3. Thread the motor bolts from the bottom of the arm, up through the arm, and into the motor base. Use the bolts that came with the motors — they are the correct length. Bolts too long will bottom out in the motor stator and damage the windings.
4. Tighten in a cross pattern (like a car wheel) to ensure even pressure. Snug but not gorilla-tight.
5. Repeat for all 4 motors.
6. Verify each motor bell spins freely after mounting. If a bell feels tight or grinds, a bolt is too long and pressing into the stator.

Pro tip: Measure your motor bolts against the motor mounting depth before installation. The bolt should be 1-2mm shorter than the threaded hole depth. If in doubt, add a washer under the bolt head as a spacer.

steps.title: ESC Mounting & Motor Wire Soldering
steps.description:
Mount the 4-in-1 ESC to the frame and solder the motor wires. This is the most critical soldering step — bad motor connections cause mid-air desyncs.

1. Place the ESC on the bottom standoffs using the rubber grommets (soft-mount). The grommets isolate the board from frame vibration, which improves gyro readings. Ensure the grommets are seated in the PCB mounting holes.
2. Secure with the short M3 bolts — do NOT over-tighten through grommets. The ESC should have slight give when pressed.
3. Route each motor's 3 wires to their corresponding ESC motor output pads (M1, M2, M3, M4). Motor pad assignment follows Betaflight's motor numbering — but don't worry about wire order yet. Any motor can connect to any 3 pads in any order; direction and assignment are configured in firmware.
4. Cut motor wires to length — leave 5mm extra for stress relief. Strip 2-3mm of insulation from each wire end.
5. Tin each motor wire end with solder. Tin each ESC pad.
6. Solder each wire to its pad: hold the iron on the pad for 1-2 seconds to heat it, touch solder to the junction (not the iron), let it flow, remove iron. Total contact time should be 3-4 seconds.
7. Inspect each joint — it should be shiny and concave (volcano-shaped joints indicate cold solder). Tug each wire gently to verify the joint holds.

Pro tip: Use flux on the ESC pads before tinning. The pads on quality ESCs have lead-free solder coating that can be stubborn without flux. Also, alternate which motor you're soldering — don't do all 3 wires of one motor consecutively, as the PCB heat can damage nearby components.
steps.safety_warning: Soldering produces toxic fumes. Work in a ventilated area or use a fume extractor. Do not touch the soldering iron tip or freshly soldered joints. Allow 30 seconds for joints to cool before handling.

steps.title: Flight Controller Stack Assembly
steps.description:
Mount the flight controller on top of the ESC to complete the stack. The FC connects to the ESC via pin headers or a ribbon cable.

1. If your stack uses pin headers, carefully align the FC's bottom-side header socket with the ESC's top-side header pins. Apply even pressure to seat the FC. Do NOT force it — misaligned pins will bend and break.
2. If your stack uses a cable connection, plug the cable into both boards first, then mount the FC.
3. Place rubber grommets on the FC mounting holes, then secure with M3 bolts to the standoffs. Again, soft-mount — don't crush the grommets.
4. Verify the FC is oriented correctly — the arrow printed on the board should point toward the front of the frame. If there's no arrow, the USB port typically faces the rear.
5. Check that no wires are pinched between the FC and ESC boards. Pinched wires can short and destroy both boards.

Pro tip: Before bolting down the FC, connect it to your computer via USB and verify Betaflight Configurator recognizes it. It's much easier to troubleshoot connection issues now than after everything is wired up.

steps.title: Receiver Soldering & Antenna Routing
steps.description:
Solder the ELRS receiver to the FC's spare UART and route the antenna for optimal reception.

1. Identify a free UART on the FC. Check the FC's wiring diagram (usually printed on the board or in the manual). You need TX, RX, 5V, and GND pads for the receiver.
2. Cut 4 short wires (28AWG silicone wire, ~40mm each): red (5V), black (GND), and two signal wires for TX and RX.
3. Solder wires to the receiver pads first (the receiver is smaller and harder to hold). Use helping hands or blue tack to secure it.
4. Solder the other ends to the FC UART pads: RX on the receiver goes to the TX pad on the FC, and TX on the receiver goes to the RX pad on the FC. This crossover is critical — TX/RX are always crossed between devices.
5. Mount the receiver to the frame using double-sided foam tape. Common locations: inside the frame near the rear standoff, or on top of the FC with a foam pad.
6. Route the antenna wire out through the rear of the frame. The antenna should extend at least 30mm beyond any carbon fiber — carbon blocks RF signals.
7. Secure the antenna with a zip tie to a rear standoff, angled 45 degrees from the frame for optimal 3D reception coverage.

Pro tip: Don't coil excess antenna wire — it creates a tuned inductor that changes the antenna's frequency response. If the wire is too long, route it in an L-shape along the frame arm instead.
steps.safety_warning: Soldering produces toxic fumes. Work in a ventilated area. The receiver is sensitive to static discharge — touch a grounded metal surface before handling.

steps.title: FPV Camera Installation
steps.description:
Mount the FPV camera in the frame's camera cage and connect it to the FC or VTX.

1. Slide the camera into the frame's front camera mount. Most freestyle frames use a 19mm-wide mounting slot for micro cameras. The Foxeer Micro Cat 4 fits standard micro mounts.
2. Set the camera tilt angle. For freestyle flying, 25-35 degrees of uptilt is typical. This determines how fast you need to fly to see the horizon. Start at 25 degrees if you're new — you can increase later.
3. Tighten the camera mount side bolts gently. The camera should be secure but not crushed — over-tightening can crack the camera's plastic housing.
4. Connect the camera's power and video cables. Analog cameras typically have 3 wires: power (red, 5-20V input), ground (black), and video signal (yellow/white).
5. Solder the camera wires to the FC's camera pads — many modern FCs have dedicated camera input pads labeled CAM or VIDEO IN. Power can come from a regulated 5V or 9V pad (check your camera's voltage range).

Pro tip: Use a piece of heat shrink or electrical tape to create a strain relief loop on the camera cable right behind the camera. This prevents the solder joint from being stressed when the camera gets hit in a crash.

steps.title: Video Transmitter Installation & Wiring
steps.description:
Install the VTX and connect it to the FC for video output and SmartAudio control.

1. Mount the VTX on top of the FC stack using double-sided foam tape or the frame's VTX mounting points. The SpeedyBee TX800 supports both 20x20 and 30.5x30.5 mounting patterns.
2. Connect the VTX power input to the battery voltage pad (VBAT) on the FC. The TX800 has a built-in voltage regulator and accepts 2-6S input directly.
3. Connect the video signal wire from the FC's video output pad (labeled VTX or VIDEO OUT) to the VTX's video input.
4. Connect the SmartAudio/Tramp wire from the VTX to a free UART TX pad on the FC. This allows Betaflight OSD to control VTX power and channel from your goggles.
5. Solder a ground wire between the VTX and FC if not already sharing ground through the video connection.
6. Do NOT connect the antenna yet — we'll do that in the next step. Never power on a VTX without an antenna connected, or you will burn out the power amplifier.

Pro tip: Keep the VTX as far from the FC's gyro chip as possible. VTX heat and RF interference can introduce noise into gyro readings, causing propwash oscillations. If your frame has a dedicated VTX mounting position on top of the stack, use it.
steps.safety_warning: Never power a VTX without an antenna connected. The reflected RF energy will destroy the VTX power amplifier within seconds. This is the #1 most common expensive mistake in FPV building.

steps.title: VTX Antenna & Battery Lead Installation
steps.description:
Install the VTX antenna and prepare the battery connection. These are the final hardware steps before first power-up.

1. Connect the VTX antenna to the VTX's antenna connector. For MMCX connectors, press straight down until you feel/hear a click. Do not twist — MMCX is a push-on connector.
2. Route the antenna upward through the top plate or to a dedicated antenna mount. The antenna should be as vertical as possible and away from carbon fiber for best signal.
3. Secure the antenna cable with a zip tie to prevent it from getting caught in props.
4. Solder the XT60 battery pigtail to the ESC's battery input pads (B+ and B-). These are the largest pads on the ESC, usually with pre-tinned landing pads.
5. Use heavy gauge wire (14-16 AWG) for the battery leads. Strip 4mm, tin generously, and solder with good heat — these carry the full current of all 4 motors.
6. Optionally, solder a low-ESR capacitor (25V 1000uF) across the battery pads. This smooths voltage ripples and protects electronics from voltage spikes during aggressive throttle changes.
7. Shrink-wrap or tape any exposed solder joints on the battery leads.

Pro tip: Route the battery lead out the rear of the frame so the XT60 connector sits behind the quad. This puts the battery weight on the rear, which many pilots prefer for balance. Also, a capacitor is technically optional but highly recommended — it costs $2 and prevents expensive component failures.
steps.safety_warning: Battery leads carry high current. Double-check polarity (red to B+, black to B-) before connecting a battery. Reversed polarity will instantly destroy the ESC and FC. Use a multimeter to verify if unsure.

steps.title: Smoke Test & Initial Power-Up
steps.description:
The moment of truth. Use a smoke stopper to protect your electronics during the first power-up.

1. Perform a final visual inspection of ALL solder joints. Look for:
   - Solder bridges (unintended connections between adjacent pads)
   - Cold joints (dull, grainy appearance instead of shiny)
   - Loose wires that could short against the frame or other components
2. Verify battery polarity one more time with a multimeter on the XT60 pigtail.
3. Connect your smoke stopper inline between the battery and the quad. A smoke stopper limits current — if there's a short, the indicator LED glows bright and no smoke emerges.
4. Connect the battery through the smoke stopper. Watch for:
   - Smoke stopper LED should glow dimly (normal startup current draw)
   - If LED glows bright and stays bright: DISCONNECT IMMEDIATELY — there's a short
   - If no LED glow at all: check battery connector and XT60 solder joints
5. If the smoke stopper shows normal draw, check that the FC boots (status LED blinks) and the VTX powers on (LED indicator on VTX).
6. Connect to Betaflight Configurator via USB while on battery power and verify the FC is recognized.
7. In the Motors tab, verify all 4 motors appear. Do NOT spin motors yet — props are not installed and motor direction hasn't been configured.

Pro tip: If you don't have a smoke stopper, you can make one from a 12V automotive light bulb in series with the battery lead. The bulb acts as a current limiter — bright glow means short circuit.
steps.safety_warning: Remove all propellers before first power-up. Even without prop installation, accidental motor spin during testing can cause injury from exposed motor bells spinning at high RPM.

steps.title: Betaflight Firmware Flash & Initial Configuration
steps.description:
Flash the latest Betaflight firmware and configure basic settings. This step transforms a pile of connected electronics into a flyable drone.

1. Open Betaflight Configurator on your computer. Connect the FC via USB.
2. Go to the Firmware Flasher tab. Select your FC target (check the FC manual for the exact target name — for the HAKRC F722, it's typically HAKRCF722V2).
3. Select the latest stable Betaflight release. Enable 'Full Chip Erase' for a clean flash.
4. Flash the firmware. Wait for the process to complete — do NOT disconnect during flashing.
5. After flashing, the FC reboots. Go to the Setup tab and verify the 3D model responds correctly to board movement.
6. Go to the Configuration tab and set these essentials using the CLI commands below.
7. Go to the Ports tab: enable Serial RX on the UART where your receiver is connected. Enable a VTX peripheral (SmartAudio or Tramp) on the VTX UART.
8. Save and reboot after each configuration change.
steps.safety_warning: Do not disconnect USB during firmware flashing. Interrupting a flash can brick the FC, requiring DFU recovery mode to restore.
steps.betaflight_cli:
# Betaflight CLI — Initial Setup for 5" Freestyle
# Paste these commands in the CLI tab, then type 'save'

# Motor protocol
set motor_pwm_protocol = DSHOT600
set dshot_bidir = ON
set dshot_bitbang = AUTO

# Arming
set small_angle = 180

# Battery monitoring
set vbat_min_cell_voltage = 330
set vbat_warning_cell_voltage = 350
set vbat_max_cell_voltage = 430

# Receiver protocol (ELRS uses CRSF)
set serialrx_provider = CRSF

# Beeper
set beeper_dshot_beacon_tone = 3

# OSD basics
set osd_vbat_pos = 2433
set osd_rssi_pos = 2105
set osd_flymode_pos = 2457
set osd_craft_name_pos = 2506
set osd_warnings_pos = 2377

# Name your quad
name Apex Freestyle

save

steps.title: Motor Direction & ESC Configuration
steps.description:
Configure motor spin direction and verify the motor order matches Betaflight's expected layout. Getting this wrong means an uncontrollable quad on first throttle.

1. Ensure NO propellers are installed. This is critical — motors will spin during this test.
2. In Betaflight Configurator, go to the Motors tab.
3. Enable the motor test slider safety switch.
4. Spin each motor one at a time at minimum throttle (~1050). Note which motor spins and in which direction.
5. Betaflight expects motors in this order (viewed from above, nose pointing away from you):
   - Motor 1: Rear Right (CCW)
   - Motor 2: Front Right (CW)
   - Motor 3: Rear Left (CW)
   - Motor 4: Front Left (CCW)
6. If a motor is assigned to the wrong position, you can either:
   - Re-solder the motor wires to the correct ESC output pads, OR
   - Use the Motor Output Reordering feature in Betaflight 4.5+ (Motors tab → Custom motor mapping)
7. For any motors spinning the wrong direction, use the Betaflight CLI:
   - `resource MOTOR x NONE` then `resource MOTOR x [pin]` to remap, OR
   - In BLHeli_S/BLHeli_32 configurator, reverse the motor direction
   - With DShot, you can reverse direction in Betaflight: `set motor_direction_inverted = ...`
8. Verify all 4 motors spin in the correct direction before proceeding.
steps.safety_warning: Propellers MUST be removed during motor testing. A spinning prop can cause serious cuts, break components, and send the quad flying across the room. Always remove props before connecting to a computer.
steps.betaflight_cli:
# Motor direction (if reversals needed via DShot commands)
# Use BLHeli configurator for BLHeli_S/32 ESCs
# Or in Betaflight CLI for bidirectional DShot:
set motor_direction_inverted = OFF

# Verify motor protocol is working
set motor_pwm_protocol = DSHOT600

save

steps.title: Receiver Binding & Control Link Test
steps.description:
Bind the ELRS receiver to your transmitter and verify all control channels respond correctly.

1. Power on your radio transmitter with ELRS module. Ensure it's set to the correct binding phrase or binding mode.
2. ELRS binding options:
   - Binding phrase (recommended): Set the same binding phrase in both TX and RX firmware. They auto-bind on power-up.
   - Traditional binding: Put the RX into bind mode (usually by powering on with the bind button held, or via CLI command). Then put the TX into bind mode.
3. Power the FC with a battery. The ELRS receiver LED should blink rapidly (searching) then go solid (bound).
4. In Betaflight Configurator, go to the Receiver tab. You should see the channel bars responding to your stick movements.
5. Verify channel mapping: move each stick and confirm the correct channel responds (Roll, Pitch, Yaw, Throttle). If channels are swapped, adjust the channel map in your radio or use Betaflight's channel map setting.
6. Check that all switches and aux channels are transmitting. Set up an Arm switch on AUX1 (critical for safety — you want a dedicated arm/disarm switch).
7. In the Modes tab, assign the ARM mode to AUX1 with a defined range (e.g., 1800-2100). This means the quad only arms when the switch is in the active position.

Pro tip: Always test failsafe behavior. With the quad disarmed, turn off your radio. The receiver should trigger a failsafe within 1-2 seconds — verify in Betaflight that the failsafe is set to 'Drop' (motors stop immediately on signal loss).
steps.betaflight_cli:
# Receiver and failsafe settings
set serialrx_provider = CRSF
set serialrx_halfduplex = AUTO

# Failsafe — critical safety setting
set failsafe_procedure = DROP
set failsafe_delay = 4
set failsafe_off_delay = 10

# Arm switch on AUX1
aux 0 0 0 1700 2100 0 0

save

steps.title: PID Tuning & Rates Configuration
steps.description:
Configure PID values and stick rates for freestyle flying. Betaflight defaults are good, but these optimizations will make the quad feel significantly better.

1. Start with Betaflight's defaults — they're excellent for 5-inch builds on recent firmware versions.
2. Configure your rates (stick sensitivity). Freestyle rates are personal preference, but a good starting point:
   - RC Rate: 1.0 (center stick sensitivity)
   - Super Rate: 0.70 (endpoint sensitivity)
   - RC Expo: 0.15 (softens center stick)
   - This gives roughly 700 deg/s max rotation rate
3. Enable RPM filtering if your ESC supports bidirectional DShot (which it should with DShot600 + bidir ON). This is the single biggest improvement for clean freestyle flight.
4. Enable Dynamic Idle. This prevents motor desyncs during low-throttle maneuvers like inverted hangs.
5. Set up a PID profile and a Rate profile so you can easily switch between setups in the field.
6. The CLI commands below provide a solid starting point for 5-inch freestyle on 2207 motors with 6S.
steps.betaflight_cli:
# PID Tuning — 5" Freestyle on 2207 / 6S
# These are optimized starting points — fine-tune to taste

# PID values
set p_pitch = 47
set i_pitch = 84
set d_pitch = 46
set f_pitch = 125
set p_roll = 45
set i_roll = 80
set d_roll = 40
set f_roll = 120
set p_yaw = 45
set i_yaw = 90
set d_yaw = 0
set f_yaw = 75

# Filters (with RPM filtering enabled)
set dyn_notch_count = 3
set dyn_notch_q = 300
set dyn_notch_min_hz = 100
set dyn_notch_max_hz = 600
set gyro_lpf1_dyn_min_hz = 250
set gyro_lpf1_dyn_max_hz = 500
set dterm_lpf1_dyn_min_hz = 100
set dterm_lpf1_dyn_max_hz = 250

# Rates — freestyle
set rates_type = BETAFLIGHT
set roll_rc_rate = 100
set pitch_rc_rate = 100
set yaw_rc_rate = 100
set roll_srate = 70
set pitch_srate = 70
set yaw_srate = 60
set roll_expo = 15
set pitch_expo = 15
set yaw_expo = 15

# Dynamic idle
set dyn_idle_min_rpm = 25
set motor_idle_throttle = 5.5

save

steps.title: Final Assembly & Pre-Flight Check
steps.description:
Install propellers, perform final checks, and prepare for maiden flight. This is the last step between you and first flight.

1. Install the top plate on the frame. Route all wires and antenna cables through the appropriate slots before bolting the top plate down.
2. Secure any loose wires with zip ties. Nothing should be hanging outside the frame where props could catch it.
3. Install propellers — this is the LAST thing you do, and ONLY when you're ready to fly:
   - CW props (marked 'R' or with arrow) go on motors 2 (front right) and 3 (rear left)
   - CCW props (marked 'L' or with arrow) go on motors 1 (rear right) and 4 (front left)
   - Press-fit or screw-on depending on the prop type. Ensure each prop is fully seated.
4. Pre-flight checklist:
   - [ ] All bolts tight (frame, motors, camera)
   - [ ] No loose wires visible
   - [ ] VTX antenna connected and secured
   - [ ] Battery strap through frame slots
   - [ ] Camera angle set (25-35 degrees)
   - [ ] Arm switch tested (arms and disarms reliably)
   - [ ] Failsafe tested (radio off → motors stop)
   - [ ] Battery voltage reading correct on OSD
   - [ ] Props spinning correct direction (verify one more time without props, then install)
5. For maiden flight: choose a large open field with no people nearby. Arm, hover at head height, check for vibrations or drift. If stable, you're ready to fly.

Congratulations — you've built a freestyle quad from scratch. The first flight is always a bit nervous, but trust your build. Start gentle and work up to full throttle as confidence builds.
steps.safety_warning: Always arm your quad pointed away from yourself and others. Never carry a quad with props installed and a battery connected. If anything feels wrong during hover — drift, vibration, odd sounds — disarm immediately and investigate on the ground.

build_guides.name: 7-Inch Long Range Explorer — Horizon Scout
build_guides.description: Advanced build guide for the Horizon Scout 7-inch long range quad. This build prioritizes range, efficiency, and safety systems including GPS rescue. Covers high-current ESC wiring, GPS module integration, long-range antenna optimization, and detailed Betaflight configuration for autonomous features. Requires confident soldering skills and familiarity with Betaflight. You will finish with a GPS-equipped 7-inch quad capable of 20+ minute flights and 10+ km range.

steps.title: Long Range Component Verification
steps.description:
Long range builds demand extra attention to component compatibility. An issue that would be a minor inconvenience on a 5-inch freestyle quad — like a marginal solder joint or undersized wire — becomes a potential crash-and-lose scenario when you're 8 km from home.

1. Verify motor KV and prop combination efficiency. The 2808 1350KV motors on 7-inch props should draw 35-45A per motor at full throttle on 6S. Confirm your ESC is rated above this.
2. Check ESC current rating: 65A continuous provides 1.5x headroom over typical motor draw. This margin is essential for sustained climbs and wind fighting.
3. Verify the FC has enough free UARTs for all peripherals: UART1 for receiver, UART2 for GPS, UART3 for VTX SmartAudio. The H743 FC has 6+ UARTs so this should be fine.
4. Inspect the GPS module — verify it has a compass (magnetometer) if you plan to use GPS rescue with compass heading. The Flywoo GOKU GM10 includes a compass.
5. Weigh all components on a gram scale. Calculate expected all-up weight (AUW). A 7-inch LR quad should target 550-700g without battery, 900-1100g with Li-Ion pack.
6. Test-fit the Li-Ion battery on the frame. Larger packs need adequate strap length and CG (center of gravity) positioning.

Pro tip: Create a weight budget spreadsheet. Long range is all about efficiency — every gram you save translates to extended flight time. A 50g savings can add 2+ minutes of flight.

steps.title: Frame Assembly & Hardware Preparation
steps.description:
Assemble the 7-inch frame with extra attention to rigidity. Larger frames experience more vibration and flex than 5-inch builds, so every bolt must be secure.

1. Lay out all frame hardware: bottom plate, top plate, arms (if separate), standoffs, camera cage, and all bolts.
2. Assemble the bottom plate with standoffs. Use blue Loctite on every standoff bolt — 7-inch quads generate stronger vibrations than 5-inch.
3. Install arms if they're separate pieces. For true-X frames, ensure equal arm angles. For dead cat or stretched X geometry, verify arm positions match the frame's intended layout.
4. Install the camera cage/bumper at the front. For long range, you may want to use a more protective camera cage design — crashes far from home are expensive.
5. Verify the stack mounting pattern: 30.5x30.5mm holes should accept M3 bolts. Test-fit the ESC and FC with their mounting hardware before installing any electronics.
6. Leave the top plate off until all electronics are installed and wired.

Pro tip: For 7-inch frames, consider using nylon or rubber standoff spacers between carbon plates. The added vibration dampening helps gyro performance and extends component life.

steps.title: Motor Installation — 7-Inch Considerations
steps.description:
Mount the 2808 motors to the frame arms. Larger motors require extra care with bolt selection and thread engagement.

1. The VCI Hobby ARES 2808 uses M3 mounting bolts with 16mm bolt spacing — standard for 7-inch class motors.
2. Feed motor wires through the arm channels. On longer 7-inch arms, the wire channel is longer — ensure the wires don't get pinched or kinked.
3. Thread motor bolts through the arm and into the motor base. Critical: verify bolt length before tightening. 2808 motors have deeper stator cans than 2207s — the bolt threads should engage the motor base for at least 3mm without bottoming out.
4. Apply a drop of blue Loctite to each motor bolt. 7-inch builds vibrate more than 5-inch, and loose motor bolts are the #1 cause of mid-flight motor detachment.
5. Tighten in cross pattern. Each motor should be snug — approximately 0.5-0.7 Nm if you have a torque driver.
6. Verify bell spin: each motor should spin silently with no resistance after mounting.
7. Repeat for all 4 motors.

Pro tip: With larger motors, the orientation of the motor wire exit can matter for routing. Try to orient motors so wires exit toward the center of the quad for the cleanest routing to the ESC.

steps.title: High-Current ESC Installation & Motor Soldering
steps.description:
Install the 65A 4-in-1 ESC and solder the motor wires. High-current ESCs require bigger solder joints and careful thermal management during soldering.

1. Mount the ESC on the bottom standoffs with soft-mount rubber grommets. The Holybro Tekko32 65A uses 30.5x30.5mm mounting.
2. Route motor wires from each arm to the corresponding ESC motor pads. The pads on 65A ESCs are larger than typical 35-45A boards — this makes soldering easier.
3. Cut motor wires to length with 5mm extra slack. Strip 3mm of insulation.
4. Tin each wire and each ESC pad generously. High-current pads need more solder to create a solid thermal and electrical connection.
5. Solder technique for high-current pads: use a larger chisel tip on your iron. Hold the iron on the pad for 2-3 seconds before feeding solder. The larger copper area needs more heat to reach soldering temperature. A 60W iron is the minimum for 65A ESC pads — 80W is better.
6. Each joint should be shiny and well-flowed. Pull-test each wire. On a long-range build, a cold joint means losing the quad.
7. Repeat for all 12 motor wires (3 per motor, 4 motors).

Pro tip: If you're struggling with heat on the large pads, bump your iron to 400C for just the ESC motor pads (drop back to 350C for signal pads). Adding flux to pre-tinned pads helps them accept solder faster.
steps.safety_warning: Soldering high-current pads requires higher temperatures and longer contact times. The ESC board will get hot — support it with a silicone pad or helping hands. Avoid touching the board for 30 seconds after completing a joint.

steps.title: Flight Controller Installation & FC-ESC Connection
steps.description:
Mount the H743 flight controller and connect it to the ESC. The H743 processor is critical for long range builds — it has the processing power to run GPS rescue, RPM filtering, and HD OSD simultaneously.

1. Connect the FC to the ESC via pin headers or cable harness. Align carefully — bent pins on an H743 board are expensive mistakes.
2. Mount the FC on the standoffs above the ESC with rubber grommets. Ensure the gyro/IMU chip (typically the small square IC on the board) is not touching any standoff or wire.
3. Verify FC orientation: the arrow on the board should point toward the front of the quad. If your frame uses a non-standard orientation (90 degree rotated mount), you can compensate in Betaflight later.
4. Quick USB test: connect to Betaflight Configurator and verify the board is recognized. Check that the 3D model moves correctly when you tilt the FC.
5. Note the UART assignments on the FC silkscreen or manual — you'll need at least 3 free UARTs:
   - UART for receiver (CRSF/ELRS)
   - UART for GPS module (serial)
   - UART for VTX SmartAudio control
   The H743 typically has 6-8 UARTs, so you have plenty.

Pro tip: On H743 boards, verify the board is running the correct Betaflight target. Some H743 boards have multiple target options (e.g., IFLIGHT_BLITZ_H7 vs generic H743). Using the wrong target can result in incorrect UART pin assignments.

steps.title: GPS Module Mounting & UART Wiring
steps.description:
Install the GPS module — the key safety component that enables GPS rescue for long range flying. Proper mounting is critical for fast satellite acquisition and reliable compass heading.

1. Choose the GPS mounting location. The module should be:
   - As far from ESC, motors, and VTX as possible (electromagnetic interference degrades GPS accuracy)
   - On a raised mount or mast (10-20mm above the frame) for clearest sky view
   - Arrow on GPS module pointing toward the front of the quad (for compass calibration)
2. Common mounting options:
   - 3D printed GPS mast on a rear standoff (best option)
   - Double-sided tape directly on the top plate (acceptable but slower fix time)
   - Zip-tied to a raised platform
3. Solder the GPS wires to the FC's designated GPS UART:
   - GPS TX → FC RX pad (on GPS UART)
   - GPS RX → FC TX pad (on GPS UART)
   - GPS VCC → 5V pad on FC
   - GPS GND → GND pad on FC
   - If compass (I2C): GPS SDA → FC SDA, GPS SCL → FC SCL
4. Route the GPS cable cleanly along the frame. Keep it away from ESC power traces and motor wires.
5. After wiring, power the FC and check that the GPS LED blinks (searching for satellites). A first-fix with clear sky view should take 30-90 seconds.

Pro tip: The GPS module's compass is extremely sensitive to electromagnetic interference. If you mount it near the ESC or motor wires, compass heading will be unreliable and GPS rescue will fly erratically. When in doubt, mount it higher on a longer mast.

steps.title: ELRS Receiver — Diversity Antenna Setup
steps.description:
Install the true diversity ELRS receiver for maximum control link reliability at range. On a long range build, the receiver antenna setup is just as critical as the GPS.

1. Solder the ELRS receiver wires to the FC's receiver UART:
   - RX TX → FC RX pad
   - RX RX → FC TX pad
   - 5V and GND to regulated 5V pads
2. Mount the receiver inside the frame using foam tape. Position it centrally for balanced antenna routing.
3. Antenna routing for true diversity (2 antennas):
   - Route antenna 1 along the rear of the frame, extending 30mm+ past any carbon
   - Route antenna 2 at 90 degrees to antenna 1 (e.g., one horizontal, one vertical)
   - This 90-degree diversity pattern ensures coverage regardless of quad orientation
4. Secure antennas with small zip ties at stress points. Leave the active element (the stripped section at the tip) free — don't tape or zip-tie over the antenna tip.
5. Verify antenna routing doesn't cross or run parallel to motor wires or VTX antenna cable — these generate interference.

Pro tip: For ultra-long range, use 900MHz ELRS instead of 2.4GHz. The 900MHz variant has significantly better penetration through obstacles and longer range, though it requires longer antennas (~82mm vs ~31mm active element).

steps.title: FPV Camera & VTX Installation
steps.description:
Install the camera and digital video transmitter. Long range builds benefit from higher VTX power output for video link integrity at distance.

1. Mount the FPV camera in the frame's camera cage. For long range cruising, set camera tilt to 15-20 degrees (lower than freestyle — you'll be flying more level).
2. Connect camera power and video signal to the FC's camera pads.
3. Mount the HDZero Freestyle V2 VTX on top of the stack or in a dedicated VTX bay.
4. Wire the VTX:
   - VBAT for power (HDZero accepts wide voltage input)
   - Video signal from FC video output pad
   - SmartAudio/UART for OSD control
5. For long range, the VTX power output matters. HDZero supports up to 1W (1000mW) output. Configure the appropriate power level for your region's regulations.
6. Mount the VTX antenna connector pointing upward. For digital systems like HDZero, antenna quality directly impacts video range.

Pro tip: For long range flying, consider a directional patch antenna on your goggles paired with an omni on the quad. This combination can double your effective video range compared to omni-to-omni.
steps.safety_warning: Never power a VTX without an antenna connected. This is especially important for high-power VTX units (1W+) — the reflected RF energy will instantly destroy the power amplifier chip.

steps.title: Battery Lead & Capacitor Installation
steps.description:
Wire the battery connection and install filtering capacitors. On a 7-inch build, the higher current draw makes a capacitor essential, not optional.

1. Solder the XT60 battery pigtail to the ESC's battery pads (B+ and B-). Use 14AWG silicone wire for the battery leads — 7-inch quads draw 100-160A peak.
2. Pre-tin both the wire ends and ESC pads before mating them. Good thermal mass is critical here — cold joints on battery pads cause voltage sag under load.
3. Solder a 1000-2200uF 35V low-ESR capacitor directly across the battery pads. For 7-inch builds, go larger (1500-2200uF) to handle the bigger current transients.
4. Keep capacitor leads as short as possible — long leads add inductance and reduce filtering effectiveness.
5. Shrink-wrap the XT60 solder joints. Also shrink-wrap the capacitor body if it could contact any carbon fiber (carbon is conductive).
6. Route the battery lead out the back or bottom of the frame depending on your battery mounting position.

Pro tip: On long range builds, some pilots add a second XT60 connector as a field-swappable option — one for LiPo and one for Li-Ion. This lets you switch battery chemistry without re-soldering.
steps.safety_warning: Double-check capacitor polarity before soldering. Electrolytic capacitors connected in reverse will explode. The negative lead is marked with a stripe on the capacitor body.

steps.title: Wire Management & Antenna Routing
steps.description:
Clean cable routing is critical on a long range build. Loose wires catch props, add drag, and create electromagnetic interference that affects GPS and receiver performance.

1. Gather all wires and cables: GPS cable, receiver wires, camera cable, VTX video cable, SmartAudio wire.
2. Bundle parallel wires together using small zip ties every 20-30mm along the frame. Avoid bundling high-current wires (motor/battery) with signal wires (receiver/GPS).
3. Verify antenna clearance: no antenna element (GPS, RX, or VTX) should be within 10mm of motor wires, ESC, or carbon fiber.
4. Tuck any excess wire length into the stack area — don't coil it, fold it flat.
5. Final antenna positions:
   - GPS: pointing skyward on its mast, arrow forward
   - RX antenna 1: extending horizontally rearward past the frame
   - RX antenna 2: extending vertically (90 degrees from antenna 1)
   - VTX antenna: pointing upward, clear of all carbon
6. Verify no wire or zip tie can contact a prop arc. Spin each motor by hand and watch for clearance.
7. Install the top plate, routing all wires through the designated slots.

steps.title: Smoke Test & System Verification
steps.description:
Comprehensive power-up test before configuring firmware. Long range builds have more components and connections, so this step is more involved than a typical 5-inch smoke test.

1. Remove all propellers. Triple-check battery polarity with a multimeter.
2. Use a smoke stopper for first power-up. Connect battery through the smoke stopper.
3. Verify each system powers on:
   - FC: status LED blinks, USB connection works
   - ESC: all 4 motor pads show correct voltage (measure with multimeter)
   - VTX: VTX LED indicates powered-on state
   - Camera: video feed visible on goggles (check channel/frequency match)
   - GPS: GPS LED blinks (searching for satellites)
   - Receiver: RX LED indicates bound state (solid green on most ELRS RX)
4. Connect to Betaflight Configurator. Go to the Receiver tab — verify stick inputs respond.
5. Go to the Motors tab — verify all 4 motors appear and respond to the test slider (without props).
6. Check voltage telemetry: the battery voltage shown in Betaflight should match your multimeter reading within 0.2V.
7. Take the quad outside with a battery for 2 minutes. Verify the GPS module acquires satellite fix (>8 satellites for reliable GPS rescue).

Pro tip: Test GPS satellite acquisition before final assembly. If the GPS can't get a fix, reposition the module. It's much easier to adjust mounting now than after everything is zip-tied and top-plated.
steps.safety_warning: All propellers must be removed during any bench testing. Even without props, test motor spin carefully — ensure fingers, wires, and loose items are clear of motor bells.

steps.title: Betaflight Initial Configuration — Long Range Profile
steps.description:
Configure Betaflight for long range flying. This involves lower PID values for efficiency, GPS setup, and conservative safety settings.

1. Flash the latest stable Betaflight firmware to the H743 FC. Use the correct target for your board.
2. Go to the Ports tab:
   - Enable Serial RX on the receiver UART
   - Enable GPS on the GPS UART (set baud rate to 115200 for M10 GPS modules)
   - Enable Peripherals → SmartAudio on the VTX UART
3. In the Configuration tab:
   - Enable GPS feature
   - Enable GPS Rescue feature (this is your safety net)
   - Enable magnetometer (compass) if your GPS has one
   - Set motor protocol to DSHOT600 with bidirectional DShot
4. Apply the CLI configuration below — it's optimized for smooth, efficient 7-inch long range flying with conservative safety margins.
steps.betaflight_cli:
# Betaflight CLI — 7" Long Range Configuration
# Flash target: IFLIGHT_BLITZ_H7 (or your specific board target)

# Motor protocol
set motor_pwm_protocol = DSHOT600
set dshot_bidir = ON
set dshot_bitbang = AUTO

# Arming
set small_angle = 180

# Battery — Li-Ion voltage thresholds (lower than LiPo)
set vbat_min_cell_voltage = 290
set vbat_warning_cell_voltage = 310
set vbat_max_cell_voltage = 420

# Receiver
set serialrx_provider = CRSF

# GPS configuration
set gps_provider = UBLOX
set gps_sbas_mode = AUTO
set gps_auto_baud = ON
set gps_auto_config = ON
set gps_ublox_use_galileo = ON
set gps_set_home_point_once = ON

# Compass calibration (run 'calibrate mag' after setup)
set mag_hardware = AUTO
set align_mag = DEFAULT

# Dynamic idle (critical for efficiency)
set dyn_idle_min_rpm = 20
set motor_idle_throttle = 4.5

# Beeper
set beeper_dshot_beacon_tone = 3

# OSD — long range essentials
set osd_vbat_pos = 2433
set osd_rssi_pos = 2105
set osd_gps_speed_pos = 2369
set osd_gps_sats_pos = 2073
set osd_home_dist_pos = 2393
set osd_home_dir_pos = 2266
set osd_altitude_pos = 2345
set osd_flymode_pos = 2457
set osd_craft_name_pos = 2506
set osd_warnings_pos = 2377
set osd_avg_cell_voltage_pos = 2465
set osd_flight_dist_pos = 2425
set osd_gps_lon_pos = 0
set osd_gps_lat_pos = 0

# Name
name Horizon Scout

save

steps.title: GPS Rescue & Failsafe Configuration
steps.description:
Configure GPS rescue — the most important safety feature on a long range build. When properly configured, GPS rescue will autonomously fly the quad back to the launch point if control link is lost.

1. In Betaflight Configurator, enable GPS Rescue in the Failsafe tab.
2. Go outside and verify GPS has a solid fix (10+ satellites) before configuring.
3. Configure GPS Rescue parameters via CLI (below). Key settings explained:
   - Rescue altitude: The altitude the quad climbs to before returning. Set this above any nearby obstacles.
   - Minimum satellites: The minimum GPS fix quality required to attempt rescue. 8 is the minimum for reliability.
   - Sanity checks: Enable these — they prevent GPS rescue from activating on bad data.
4. Configure failsafe behavior:
   - Stage 1 (signal lost): Enter GPS rescue after a 1-second delay
   - Stage 2 (GPS rescue fails): Emergency landing (motors off)
5. Test GPS rescue on the bench: arm the quad (no props), verify you can trigger GPS rescue via failsafe simulation in Betaflight. The motors should spool up slightly (to climb) before disarming due to no GPS movement.
6. After maiden flight, test GPS rescue at low altitude (10m) over a soft field. Deliberately disable your radio, watch the quad climb and return, then re-enable radio to take back control.

GPS rescue is not a guarantee — it fails if satellites are blocked, compass is interfered, or battery is too low. But it has saved thousands of quads from being lost. Configure it correctly and it becomes your most valuable safety net.
steps.safety_warning: Never rely solely on GPS rescue. It requires good satellite coverage, calibrated compass, and sufficient battery to fly home. Always maintain visual awareness of your quad's position and have a plan for manual recovery if GPS rescue fails.
steps.betaflight_cli:
# GPS Rescue Configuration
set gps_rescue_min_sats = 8
set gps_rescue_angle = 32
set gps_rescue_initial_alt = 50
set gps_rescue_descent_dist = 200
set gps_rescue_ground_speed = 750
set gps_rescue_throttle_min = 1200
set gps_rescue_throttle_max = 1700
set gps_rescue_throttle_hover = 1350
set gps_rescue_sanity_checks = RESCUE_SANITY_ON
set gps_rescue_allow_arming_without_fix = OFF
set gps_rescue_alt_mode = MAX_ALT
set gps_rescue_use_mag = ON

# Failsafe — triggers GPS rescue on signal loss
set failsafe_procedure = GPS-RESCUE
set failsafe_delay = 10
set failsafe_off_delay = 200
set failsafe_recovery_delay = 20

# Aux switch for GPS rescue (manual activation)
aux 1 46 1 1700 2100 0 0

save

steps.title: PID Tuning — Efficiency-Optimized Long Range
steps.description:
Long range PID tuning prioritizes smooth, efficient flight over aggressive responsiveness. Lower D-term values reduce motor heat and power consumption. Softer rates keep flight smooth for cinematic footage.

1. Start with Betaflight defaults, then apply the adjustments below.
2. Key differences from freestyle tuning:
   - Lower P and D values: 7-inch props have more inertia, requiring less aggressive correction
   - Higher I term: Compensates for wind gusts and drift during long cruises
   - Lower rates: Max rotation speed of 500-600 deg/s is plenty for long range
   - More expo: Softens center-stick for smooth camera movements
3. Enable RPM filtering for clean motor performance.
4. Set up two rate profiles: Rate Profile 1 for cruising (smooth, low rates), Rate Profile 2 for more aggressive flying if needed.

Pro tip: After maiden flight, review the blackbox log for any oscillations. 7-inch builds are more sensitive to propwash than 5-inch due to larger prop wash vortices. Increase D-term by 5-10 if you see post-flip oscillations.
steps.betaflight_cli:
# PID Tuning — 7" Long Range on 2808 / 6S

# PID Profile 1: Long Range Cruise
set p_pitch = 40
set i_pitch = 90
set d_pitch = 35
set f_pitch = 100
set p_roll = 38
set i_roll = 85
set d_roll = 32
set f_roll = 95
set p_yaw = 35
set i_yaw = 95
set d_yaw = 0
set f_yaw = 60

# Filters — conservative for clean signal
set dyn_notch_count = 3
set dyn_notch_q = 350
set dyn_notch_min_hz = 80
set dyn_notch_max_hz = 500
set gyro_lpf1_dyn_min_hz = 200
set gyro_lpf1_dyn_max_hz = 450
set dterm_lpf1_dyn_min_hz = 80
set dterm_lpf1_dyn_max_hz = 200

# Rates — smooth long range cruising
set rates_type = BETAFLIGHT
set roll_rc_rate = 90
set pitch_rc_rate = 90
set yaw_rc_rate = 85
set roll_srate = 55
set pitch_srate = 55
set yaw_srate = 45
set roll_expo = 25
set pitch_expo = 25
set yaw_expo = 20

# Antigravity — compensates for throttle changes
set anti_gravity_gain = 5000

save

steps.title: Final Assembly, Balance Check & Maiden Prep
steps.description:
Complete the physical build, verify center of gravity, and prepare for the maiden flight. Long range maiden flights require extra planning compared to a park freestyle session.

1. Install all remaining hardware: top plate, battery strap, any protective TPU mounts.
2. Install propellers LAST:
   - 2808 motors typically use 7-inch props with 5mm center holes
   - CW props on motors 2 and 3, CCW props on motors 1 and 4
   - Verify prop rotation matches Betaflight motor direction
3. Mount a battery and check the center of gravity (CG). The quad should balance roughly at the center of the frame when held by the battery strap. If nose-heavy, slide the battery backward. If tail-heavy, slide forward.
4. Weigh the complete quad with battery. Record the all-up weight (AUW) — this is your reference for flight time calculations.
5. Pre-maiden checklist:
   - [ ] All bolts tight and Loctited
   - [ ] No loose wires in prop arc
   - [ ] GPS getting 10+ satellite fix
   - [ ] ELRS link solid (RSSI -30 to -50 dBm at 10m)
   - [ ] Failsafe tested (radio off → GPS rescue activates)
   - [ ] Compass calibrated (rotate quad in all axes when prompted)
   - [ ] Battery voltage correct on OSD
   - [ ] Camera feed clear and oriented correctly
   - [ ] All motor directions verified (no props → motor test)
   - [ ] Props CW/CCW on correct motors
6. Maiden flight location: large open field, no people, good GPS reception (away from buildings/trees). Bring a fully charged radio, 2 batteries, and a spotter if possible.
7. First hover: take off gently to 3m, hover for 30 seconds. Check for vibrations, drift, or unusual behavior. Land and review.
8. Second flight: climb to 20m, test GPS rescue at low altitude. If rescue works, proceed to normal flying.

Congratulations — you've built a GPS-equipped long range explorer. Respect the machine's range capability. Start with conservative flights (1-2 km) and work outward as you build confidence in the GPS rescue and control link.
steps.safety_warning: Long range flying carries elevated risk. Always maintain awareness of battery voltage, GPS satellite count, and RSSI. Set conservative voltage alarms (3.3V/cell for Li-Ion). Never fly beyond your ability to recover the quad on foot. Comply with all local aviation regulations regarding flight altitude and visual line of sight requirements.

build_guides.name: 5-Inch HD Digital Build — Cloudwalker HD
build_guides.description: Step-by-step guide for building the Cloudwalker HD 5-inch digital FPV quad. This build features an HD digital video system (Walksnail Avatar) for cinema-quality FPV footage, paired with a reliable Axisflying stack and true diversity ELRS receiver. Covers the unique considerations of digital builds — heavier camera/VTX, different wiring, and digital-specific Betaflight configuration. Ideal for intermediate builders who want to step up from analog to HD digital video.

steps.title: HD Build Component Inventory
steps.description:
A digital HD build has different weight and power considerations than analog. Before assembly, understand how the HD system affects the build.

1. Lay out all components. Pay special attention to the HD camera and VTX:
   - The Walksnail Avatar HD Pro camera is heavier than analog cameras (~9g vs ~5g for analog)
   - The VTX unit may be larger than analog VTX and requires specific mounting
   - The camera cable/connector may be proprietary (check compatibility)
2. Inspect the FlyFishRC Volador II VX5 O4 Pro frame — this frame is specifically designed with HD camera mounting. Verify the camera cage dimensions match the Walksnail camera.
3. Check the Axisflying Argus ECO stack:
   - F4 FC + 60A ESC combo
   - 30.5x30.5mm mounting pattern (verify against frame)
   - Pin header or cable connection between FC and ESC
4. Verify battery specs: HD systems draw more power than analog (additional 10-15W for the digital VTX). The 6S 1480mAh battery should provide 4-5 minutes of aggressive freestyle.
5. Count all hardware, bolts, and grommets.

Pro tip: Weigh the HD camera + VTX combo and compare to an analog equivalent. The weight difference (typically 15-30g extra) affects the quad's handling. You may want slightly lower rates or more I-term to compensate.

steps.title: Frame Assembly — HD Camera Mount
steps.description:
Assemble the Volador II VX5 frame with attention to the HD-specific camera mounting system.

1. Start with the bottom plate. Install standoffs with blue Loctite as described in the standard frame assembly process.
2. The Volador II VX5 O4 Pro has a camera cage designed for the wider 20mm mounting pattern used by DJI/Walksnail cameras (standard analog cameras use 19mm). Verify the camera cage accepts the Walksnail Avatar camera.
3. Do NOT install the camera yet — we need to run wires through the front area first.
4. Install the arms if separate. The Volador II uses a standard true-X layout.
5. Install motor mounting hardware but leave the top plate off.

Pro tip: Check if the Volador II includes both 19mm and 20mm camera side plates. Some HD frames ship with both sizes, letting you swap between analog and digital cameras later.

steps.title: Motor Installation
steps.description:
Install all four 2207.5 motors. Same process as any 5-inch build, but the slightly taller stator height of the 2207.5 means you should verify bolt depth.

1. Feed motor wires through each arm's wire channel.
2. Mount each motor with M3 16mm-spacing bolts. The Axisflying AF2207.5 has a slightly deeper motor base than standard 2207 — verify bolt length won't bottom out.
3. Apply Loctite to motor bolts. Tighten in cross pattern.
4. Spin test each bell after mounting — smooth and silent rotation.
5. Repeat for all 4 motors.

Pro tip: Orient motor wire exits consistently. Having all 4 motors' wires exit the same direction (toward the frame center) makes routing and soldering much cleaner.

steps.title: Stack Installation & Motor Soldering
steps.description:
Mount the Axisflying Argus ECO stack (F4 FC + 60A ESC) and solder motor wires. The 60A rating provides excellent headroom for the 2207.5 motors on 6S.

1. Mount the ESC on the bottom standoffs with rubber grommets (soft-mount).
2. Route motor wires to the ESC motor pads (M1-M4). Cut to length with 5mm slack.
3. Tin and solder each motor wire to its ESC pad. The 60A ESC pads should accept solder readily at 370C.
4. After all 12 motor wires are soldered, inspect every joint carefully. Pull-test each one.
5. Stack the FC on top of the ESC via the pin header connection. Secure with grommets and M3 bolts.
6. Quick USB test — connect to Betaflight, verify the FC is recognized.

Pro tip: The Argus ECO stack uses the standard Axisflying pinout. Double-check the motor pad layout against the silkscreen — some stacks label M1-M4 differently than Betaflight's default motor order.
steps.safety_warning: Soldering produces toxic fumes. Work in a ventilated area. Allow joints to cool before handling. The 60A ESC pads require adequate heat — underpowered irons create cold joints.

steps.title: HD Camera & VTX System Installation
steps.description:
Install the Walksnail Avatar HD Pro camera and HDZero VTX. This is where HD builds diverge significantly from analog.

1. Mount the Walksnail Avatar HD Pro camera in the frame's camera cage. Set tilt to 25-30 degrees for freestyle.
2. Secure the camera with side bolts. The wider 20mm mounting requires the correct camera cage plates.
3. The Walksnail system uses a single cable connecting the camera to the VTX unit. Route this cable through the frame's center channel, keeping it away from ESC pads and motor wires.
4. Mount the VTX unit. Options depend on the frame:
   - Stack-mounted: On top of the FC stack if the frame has space
   - Rear-mounted: Behind the stack, secured with VHB tape
   - The HDZero Freestyle V2 supports 20x20 and 30.5x30.5 mounting
5. Wire the VTX power: connect to VBAT (battery voltage) pads on the FC. HD VTX units draw more current than analog — typically 1-2A at full power, so ensure the VBAT pad can handle it.
6. The camera-to-VTX connection on digital systems is typically a proprietary cable (not a standard video signal wire like analog). Just plug in the included cable between camera and VTX.
7. The UART connection for OSD/settings control varies by digital system:
   - Walksnail: Uses MSP DisplayPort for OSD (requires UART TX and RX)
   - HDZero: Uses MSP DisplayPort for OSD
   - Configure the appropriate UART in Betaflight Ports tab

Pro tip: HD VTX units generate more heat than analog. Ensure adequate airflow around the VTX. Some pilots add copper heat sinks or thermal pads between the VTX and frame to dissipate heat.
steps.safety_warning: Digital VTX units output higher power than most analog VTX. Always connect the antenna before powering on. Some HD VTX units can output 1.2W+ which will damage the PA chip instantly without an antenna load.

steps.title: ELRS True Diversity Receiver Installation
steps.description:
Install the iFlight ELRS True Diversity 2.4GHz receiver. True diversity gives you two antennas and two receiver chains — the system always uses the stronger signal, providing superior reception compared to single-antenna setups.

1. Solder the receiver wires to the FC:
   - RX TX → FC RX pad (on your chosen UART)
   - RX RX → FC TX pad (crossed — this is correct)
   - 5V → regulated 5V pad
   - GND → ground pad
2. Mount the receiver centrally in the frame using foam tape.
3. Route the two diversity antennas:
   - Antenna 1: along the rear, extending past the frame, oriented horizontally
   - Antenna 2: perpendicular to antenna 1 (e.g., vertically along a rear standoff)
   - The 90-degree offset maximizes spatial diversity coverage
4. Secure each antenna with a zip tie at the exit point from the frame. Leave the active tip elements free and unobstructed.
5. Verify both antennas are clear of carbon fiber, motor wires, and the VTX antenna.

Pro tip: With true diversity, even aggressive freestyle moves (rolls, flips) that would momentarily blank a single antenna are covered by the second antenna. This eliminates the 'control hiccups' some pilots experience with single-antenna ELRS setups.

steps.title: VTX Antenna & Battery Lead
steps.description:
Install the VTX antenna and battery connection. For HD builds, the antenna quality matters more because digital video is more bandwidth-sensitive than analog.

1. Connect the Lumenier AXII 2 antenna to the VTX's antenna connector. The AXII 2 is a premium circularly polarized antenna (RHCP) that provides excellent signal quality for digital video.
2. Mount the antenna vertically, extending above the top plate. Use a zip tie or TPU antenna mount.
3. Ensure the antenna RHCP polarization matches your goggles' antenna polarization. Mismatched polarization (RHCP on quad, LHCP on goggles) causes massive signal loss.
4. Solder the XT60 battery pigtail to the ESC battery pads. Use 14-16AWG wire.
5. Optionally install a low-ESR capacitor (1000uF 35V) across battery pads.
6. Shrink-wrap all exposed battery lead solder joints.

Pro tip: For digital systems, antenna quality has a much larger impact on range and video quality than analog. A $15 premium antenna like the AXII 2 will outperform a $3 stock whip antenna by 30-50% in range.
steps.safety_warning: Verify battery lead polarity with a multimeter before first connection. The 60A ESC has no reverse polarity protection — reversed leads will destroy the ESC and FC instantly.

steps.title: Smoke Test & HD Video Link Verification
steps.description:
Power-up test with special attention to the HD video system. Digital video requires additional verification steps compared to analog.

1. Remove all propellers. Use smoke stopper for first power-up.
2. Connect battery through smoke stopper. Verify normal current draw.
3. HD-specific checks:
   - VTX LED indicates powered-on state (most digital VTX show specific LED patterns)
   - Camera LED indicates active video capture
   - Put on your goggles — you should see a live camera feed with OSD elements
   - Verify video resolution is correct (1080p for Walksnail, 720p/1080p for HDZero)
   - Check for video artifacts, freezing, or lag — these indicate a bad camera-VTX cable
4. Connect to Betaflight Configurator via USB:
   - Verify FC recognized
   - Check receiver input (sticks respond)
   - Verify OSD elements display correctly on the goggles' OSD
5. If OSD doesn't appear, check the UART configuration — digital OSD uses MSP DisplayPort, not the analog OSD chip. Enable 'MSP DisplayPort' on the correct UART in the Ports tab.

Pro tip: If you see 'digital noise' or static in the HD feed, the camera cable may be routed too close to the ESC power traces. Re-route the cable away from high-current paths.
steps.safety_warning: Remove all propellers during testing. Never power on with antenna disconnected.

steps.title: Betaflight Configuration — HD Digital Profile
steps.description:
Configure Betaflight with HD-specific settings. Digital video systems require different OSD configuration and may need specific UART settings.

1. Flash the latest Betaflight firmware for your FC target.
2. Configure Ports:
   - Serial RX on receiver UART
   - MSP on the camera/VTX UART (for digital OSD — this replaces the analog OSD chip configuration)
3. Digital OSD configuration:
   - For Walksnail: Enable 'MSP DisplayPort' peripheral on the VTX UART
   - For HDZero: Same process — MSP DisplayPort
   - The OSD elements are configured in Betaflight just like analog, but rendered by the digital system
4. Apply the CLI configuration below — optimized for 5-inch HD digital freestyle.
steps.betaflight_cli:
# Betaflight CLI — 5" HD Digital Freestyle

# Motor protocol
set motor_pwm_protocol = DSHOT600
set dshot_bidir = ON
set dshot_bitbang = AUTO

# Arming
set small_angle = 180

# Battery (LiPo)
set vbat_min_cell_voltage = 330
set vbat_warning_cell_voltage = 350
set vbat_max_cell_voltage = 430

# Receiver (ELRS/CRSF)
set serialrx_provider = CRSF

# Digital OSD — use MSP DisplayPort
# (Configure in Ports tab: enable MSP on VTX UART)

# OSD layout — optimized for HD resolution
set osd_vbat_pos = 2433
set osd_rssi_pos = 2105
set osd_flymode_pos = 2457
set osd_craft_name_pos = 2506
set osd_warnings_pos = 2377
set osd_avg_cell_voltage_pos = 2465
set osd_timer_1_pos = 2497

# Beeper
set beeper_dshot_beacon_tone = 3

# Dynamic idle
set dyn_idle_min_rpm = 25
set motor_idle_throttle = 5.5

# Name
name Cloudwalker HD

save

steps.title: Motor Configuration & Direction Check
steps.description:
Verify motor order and spin direction. Same process as any build, but with the HD system running you can verify the OSD simultaneously.

1. Remove all propellers. Connect battery.
2. In Betaflight Motors tab, enable the test slider.
3. Spin each motor one at a time. Verify:
   - Motor 1 (rear right) spins CCW
   - Motor 2 (front right) spins CW
   - Motor 3 (rear left) spins CW
   - Motor 4 (front left) spins CCW
4. If motor direction is wrong, reverse via BLHeli configurator or Betaflight DShot commands.
5. While motors are being tested, verify on your goggles that the OSD is displaying correctly — battery voltage, RSSI, and flight mode should all be visible.
6. Bind the receiver if not already done. Verify all stick channels and aux switches.
7. Set up the Arm switch on AUX1. Test arm/disarm reliability.
8. Configure failsafe: set to DROP or GPS-RESCUE depending on your preference.

Pro tip: With an HD system, check the OSD for any latency. The Betaflight stick position display (Receiver tab) should match your physical stick position with no perceptible delay. If there's delay, check the UART configuration.
steps.safety_warning: Propellers must be removed during motor testing. Verify all props are removed before enabling the motor test slider in Betaflight.
steps.betaflight_cli:
# Failsafe
set failsafe_procedure = DROP
set failsafe_delay = 4

# Arm on AUX1
aux 0 0 0 1700 2100 0 0

save

steps.title: PID Tuning — HD Weight Compensation
steps.description:
HD builds are typically 20-40g heavier than analog equivalents. This affects flight characteristics and requires slightly different PID values.

1. The extra weight of the HD camera and VTX shifts the center of gravity slightly forward and increases inertia. This means:
   - Slightly higher P and I values compensate for the added weight
   - The quad may feel 'mushier' on default PIDs — boosting P and F terms helps
2. Apply the CLI configuration below — it's the 5-inch freestyle base with HD weight adjustments.
3. Configure rates to your preference. The HD weight doesn't significantly change rate feel.
4. Enable RPM filtering for the cleanest possible HD footage. Motor vibrations visible on analog video are absolutely unacceptable on HD — every vibration shows in 1080p.

Pro tip: HD footage reveals every vibration. Spend extra time on PID tuning and motor soft-mounting. Consider using softer motor mounting grommets and ensuring all motor bolts are evenly torqued. The difference between 'good enough' analog and 'good enough' HD tuning is significant.
steps.betaflight_cli:
# PID Tuning — 5" HD Digital (heavier build)

set p_pitch = 50
set i_pitch = 88
set d_pitch = 48
set f_pitch = 130
set p_roll = 48
set i_roll = 84
set d_roll = 42
set f_roll = 125
set p_yaw = 48
set i_yaw = 92
set d_yaw = 0
set f_yaw = 80

# Filters
set dyn_notch_count = 3
set dyn_notch_q = 300
set dyn_notch_min_hz = 100
set dyn_notch_max_hz = 600
set gyro_lpf1_dyn_min_hz = 250
set gyro_lpf1_dyn_max_hz = 500
set dterm_lpf1_dyn_min_hz = 100
set dterm_lpf1_dyn_max_hz = 250

# Rates — freestyle
set rates_type = BETAFLIGHT
set roll_rc_rate = 100
set pitch_rc_rate = 100
set yaw_rc_rate = 100
set roll_srate = 70
set pitch_srate = 70
set yaw_srate = 60
set roll_expo = 15
set pitch_expo = 15
set yaw_expo = 15

# Dynamic idle
set dyn_idle_min_rpm = 25
set motor_idle_throttle = 5.5

save

steps.title: Final Assembly & Pre-Flight Check
steps.description:
Complete the build, install props, and prepare for maiden flight. HD builds have one extra consideration — verify video recording if your system supports DVR.

1. Install the top plate. Route all wires cleanly through the frame's designated slots.
2. Secure loose wires with zip ties. Double-check that no wires cross prop arcs.
3. Install propellers (LAST step before flight):
   - CW props on motors 2 and 3
   - CCW props on motors 1 and 4
   - Ensure full seating on motor shafts
4. Mount battery and verify CG (center of gravity). HD builds tend to be slightly nose-heavy — adjust battery position to compensate.
5. HD-specific pre-flight checks:
   - [ ] Video feed clear on goggles at 30m range (walk test)
   - [ ] OSD elements displaying (voltage, RSSI, flight mode)
   - [ ] DVR recording works (if goggles support it — test record and playback)
   - [ ] No video freezing or artifacts during walk test
6. Standard pre-flight checklist:
   - [ ] All bolts tight
   - [ ] VTX and RX antennas connected and secured
   - [ ] Arm switch functions correctly
   - [ ] Failsafe tested
   - [ ] Battery voltage reading accurate on OSD
   - [ ] Props correct direction
7. For maiden: large open field, good weather, fully charged batteries. Hover at 2m, check for vibrations. If video is clean and flight is stable, you're ready.

Congratulations — you've built an HD digital freestyle quad. The video quality will be dramatically better than analog. Remember to start DVR recording before each session — HD footage from FPV looks incredible and you'll want to capture every flight.
steps.safety_warning: Always arm pointing away from yourself and others. The additional weight of HD builds means slightly more momentum in crashes — land gently while learning the quad's handling characteristics.
